coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots modified Bi2MoO6/Bi2S3 heterojunction for efficient photocatalytic removal of organic pollutants and Cr (Ⅵ)
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Pei
Chen, Zhuo
Li, Xiaoming
Wu, You
Xia, Yitian
Duan, Abing
Wang, Dongbo
Yang, Qi
coredata.dc:description:
A Bi2MoO6/carbon quantum dots/Bi2S3 (BMO/CQDs/BIS) was successfully fabricated as a high-efficient photocatalyst for degradation of some persistent organic pollutants and reduction of Cr (Ⅵ), in which the CQDs was derived from waste sludge biomass. The conversion photoluminescence and electron conductivity of CQDs enhanced the photocatalysis activity of BMO/BIS heterojunction. Compared with BIS (20.8%), BMO (63.3%) and BMO/BIS heterojunction (82.3%), the degradation efficiency of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) reached to 92.8 % by BMO/CQDs/BIS under visible light. Besides, 88 % reduction rate of hexavalent chromium Cr (Ⅵ) could be obtained. Investigation on influence factors of TC photodegradation revealed that above 90 % of TC degradation rate with a low dosage and initial pH at 4.5 could reach, and under full-spectrum light, the rate could be enhanced up to 96%. Further research indicated that it could be also used for the treatment of TC in different water quality parameters. Based on the assessment of band structure and density functional theory calculation, the effective photogenerated electron transfer could arouse more radical species to attack the chemisorption sites on the TC molecules. The main active species (holes,
O
2
-
and
OH) were identified by electron spin capture technology and typical active radicals trapping test. The intermediates of TC were detected by LC-MS and the possible mineralization pathway of TC was elaborated. This research might provide a novel approach to support waste sludge treatment and optimize photocatalyst for environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots modified Bi2MoO6/Bi2S3 heterojunction for efficient photocatalytic removal of organic pollutants and Cr (Ⅵ)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A Bi2MoO6/carbon quantum dots/Bi2S3 (BMO/CQDs/BIS) was successfully fabricated as a high-efficient photocatalyst for degradation of some persistent organic pollutants and reduction of Cr (Ⅵ), in which the CQDs was derived from waste sludge biomass. The conversion photoluminescence and electron conductivity of CQDs enhanced the photocatalysis activity of BMO/BIS heterojunction. Compared with BIS (20.8%), BMO (63.3%) and BMO/BIS heterojunction (82.3%), the degradation efficiency of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) reached to 92.8 % by BMO/CQDs/BIS under visible light. Besides, 88 % reduction rate of hexavalent chromium Cr (Ⅵ) could be obtained. Investigation on influence factors of TC photodegradation revealed that above 90 % of TC degradation rate with a low dosage and initial pH at 4.5 could reach, and under full-spectrum light, the rate could be enhanced up to 96%. Further research indicated that it could be also used for the treatment of TC in different water quality parameters. Based on the assessment of band structure and density functional theory calculation, the effective photogenerated electron transfer could arouse more radical species to attack the chemisorption sites on the TC molecules. The main active species (holes,
O
2
-
and
OH) were identified by electron spin capture technology and typical active radicals trapping test. The intermediates of TC were detected by LC-MS and the possible mineralization pathway of TC was elaborated. This research might provide a novel approach to support waste sludge treatment and optimize photocatalyst for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the increase of industrial activities has caused continuous enrichment of heavy metal and persistent organic pollutants (POPs) in the environment [1,2]. Within these harmful pollutants, the impacts of Cr6+ and residual antibiotics on water organisms and human health should not be underestimated [3]. Cr6+ is a typical heavy metal contaminant with high toxicity and high teratogenicity. Once Cr6+ enters ecological cycle chain, it will threaten human health. Meanwhile, due to abuse, the residues of TC are more likely to induce the production of biological resistance gene [4,5]. For efficient removal of TC and heavy metal, more attention has been placed on clean, green, and convenient photocatalysis technology [6,7]. It is widely admitted that the photocatalyst with abilities of efficient light-harvesting and photocarriers separation are essential to enhance photocatalysis performance [8]. Therefore, numerous researchers focus on the exploration of novel photocatalysts to keep two properties of a broad light adsorption range and fast electron transfer simultaneously.

So far, various photocatalysts have been developed such as Bi-based [9], g-C3N4-base [10], Ag-base [11]. Among them, Bi-based semiconductors due to suitable band energy, abundant crystal forms have the potential as high-efficient photocatalysts [12,13]. In Bi-based semiconductors, the Bi2MoO6 (BMO) possesses the chemical/thermal stability and the efficient charge transfer ability thanks to the [Bi2O2]2+ layered structures and high-quality perovskite [MoO4]2- octahedral crystal layers [14]. However, the pure BMO generally responses at the ultraviolet wavelength range leading to a low quantum efficiency. Many solutions have been proposed including engineering transformation of microstructure and crystal planes [15], doping of non-metallic elements [16], and formation of heterojunctions [17]. Compared with the techniques of single semiconductor modification, the BMO-based heterojunctions strategies with multi-semiconductor could broaden light response range, keep high redox ability, and accelerate photocarriers migration [18].Therefore, choosing a semiconductor that is band-matched to the BMO is crucial for building stable heterojunction structures [19,20].

As another Bi-based semiconductor, Bi2S3 (BIS) with a narrow bandgap (∼1.4 eV) has been investigated [21]. Generally, the BIS, as a good photosensitizer, could arouse more photocarriers. However, it was found that the electronic reflux in BIS would bring about photo corrosion and electron-hole pairs recombination [9]. It is possible for BMO and BIS to construct a high-efficient photocatalyst with heterojunction thereby obtaining above excellent performance and making up for the above deficiencies of materials. In addition, similar congeners would be more likely to form a stable heterojunctions structure [18]. With heterojunction strategies, the recombination effect might also occur when the electrons accumulate on the band edge position such as Type Ⅰ heterojunction. A solution that a “conductor” could be introduced to guide the electrons to the surface catalysis has been proved validly [22]. Thus, low-cost, nontoxic CQDs could meet this requirement due to their high conductivity and electron storage properties [23]. Meanwhile, the CQDs as a “conductor” have already worked in heterojunctions modification. It has been proved that CQDs can modify both narrow-band and wide-bandgap photocatalysts [24,25]. At present, the CQDs have been prepared by some common carbon-containing substances [26]. The biomass waste sludge with abundant carbohydrates like protein or other biodegradable organic substrates seems to be a potential raw material for preparing CQDs. After the purification process, more fluorescent function groups would generate on CQDs for conductivity and photoluminescence. Besides, CQDs could also act as photocatalysts to generate electron-hole pairs which also facilitated the catalytic process [27]. Moreover, it has been a hassle to handle the waste sludge due to secondary pollution [28]. It is a win–win thing to use waste sludge as a precursor for CQDs preparation. The particle size of CQDs is less than 10 nm with excellent up and down conversion photoluminescence properties [29]. The near-infrared and ultraviolet light could be converted into visible light [30]. Therefore, the CQDs are able to increase the quantum yield and accelerate the reaction process. The charge transfer on heterojunctions could be promoted to improve photocatalytic activity.

In this study, the BMO/CQDs/BIS heterojunctions with core–shell structure were prepared via a solvothermal method. The CQDs were derived from biomass waste sludge. The tests of XRD, XPS, FT-IR, SEM, TEM, and BET clarified the structural characteristics of as-prepared materials. And the optical properties were revealed by PL, UV–vis and photochemical experiments. Meanwhile, the TC and Cr(Ⅵ) were employed as typical POPs and heavy metals respectively in the photocatalysis efficiency experiment, and the photodegradation of different typical POPs (DOX, OTC, and CIP) was also investigated. The effects of influences factors on TC photodegradation, including pH, initial pollutant concentration, different catalyst dosages, actual water sources and supporting electrolytes were also studied. Besides, active free radicals involved in the reaction were analyzed. Finally, the reaction mechanisms, including band structure calculation of BMO/CQDs/BIS and predicting reaction attack sites of TC molecules, were explored. And the possible photocatalysis degradation pathway of TC was explained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process of core–shell structure BMO/CQDs/BIS
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the CQDs were obtained by a modified hydrothermal method [31]. Specifically, the 2.0 g biomass waste sewage sludge was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water. After 10 min ultrasound reaction, the commixtures of water and mud were stirred evenly at room temperature. After the hydrothermal reaction process (180 °C and 12 h), the products were centrifuged thrice at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitated impurities were filtered out by a 0.45 μm micron filter to obtain yellow–brown upper liquid. The prepared CQDs liquid was stored at low temperatures (0–4 ℃) and dark environment for the next compound preparation.

Secondly, the CQDs/BIS nanosphere was fabricated as the core in the core–shell structure [32]. The solvent A was a 1.94 g bismuth nitrate pentahydrate in 30 mL DMF, and 0.727 g L-cysteine was dissolved in 30 mL DMF as solvent B. Then, B was slowly poured into A stirring 30 min with the 3 mL CQDs. The obtained mixtures were added into the polyethylene inner reactor, heating at 180 °C for 8 h. The black CQDs/BIS composites powder were purified by ethanol and deionized water to remove the residual organic matter in 2–3 times. The as-prepared samples were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight. Meanwhile, the BIS nanosphere was obtained by similar procedure without adding CQDs.

Finally, the BMO/CQDs/BIS nanocomposite (n (BIS: BMO) = 1:3) was obtained by a simple solvothermal process. The 1 mL HNO3 (5 mol/L), Na2MoO6 (0.242 g), and CQDs/BIS (0.727 g) were totally dissolved in 45 mL ethanol and then the mixtures of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O (0.972 g) and ethylene glycol (25 mL) were added slowly. After the mixed solvent was stirred for 1 h, the final solution was heated at 160 °C for 24 h. After the purification process, the BMO/CQDs/BIS were dried at 60 °C overnight. Then, the structure characterization of all prepared samples was tested and detailed in supporting information. The preparation process of the BMO/CQDs/BIS heterojunction catalyst was shown in Scheme 1 .

The photocatalytic activity of prepared materials was evaluated by various pollutants under visible light, which the 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter (light intensity: 100 mW/cm2) was used as a light source. 30 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in TC, DOX, OTC, and CIP solutions (100 mL, 20 mg/L) respectively, then the photocatalyst and Cr (Ⅵ) (20 mg/L) were mixed in 100 mL aqueous solution. After the adsorption–desorption equilibrium in the darkness, the solutions were irradiated for 120 min at 25 °C. During the illumination process, 3 mL of the reaction solution was extracted at certain time intervals, filtering with a 0.45 μm filter head. The concentration of TC was identified by a Shimadzu Uv–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of frontier electron density function
ce:section.ce:para: The point charges (natural) of TC molecule were calculated by DFT at B3LYP/6-31G* level with diffusion in water as the solvent using the Guassian 09 program. Based on frontier orbital theory, the θA HOMO and θA LUMO were directly exported by Multiwfn [33], which represented the frontier electron densities [34] contribution values of atom A on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Therefore, the corresponding values of 2θA HOMO = 2FEDHOMO 2 and θA HOMO + θA LUMO = FED2 HOMO + FED2 LUMO could reveal the electrophilic reaction and nucleophilic reaction respectively. Therefore, the attack sites from atom of TC molecule could be predicted to identify the chemisorption position and process on BMO/CQDs/BIS.

The crystalline nature and phase information of prepared samples were showcased in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra (Fig. 1 ). All peaks from BIS could be accurately indexed to JCPDS 65–2435 (Fig.S1). Likewise, the pure BMO diffraction peaks at about 28.24°,32.49°,46.66°,47.07° and 55.37° were assigned to (131), (002), (202), (212), (133) crystal planes of orthogonal BMO (JCPDS 84–0787) (Fig.S1), respectively. The XRD pattern of BMO/BIS and BMO/CQDs/BIS were similar to that of pure BMO. The results suggested that good crystals structure of BIS and BMO were not affected by CQDs [35]. Meanwhile, compared with BMO/BIS, the BMO/CQDs/BIS diffraction peaks were sharper and narrower, indicating that sample size increased and crystallinity were changed after CQDs were implanted [36]. No peaks of CQDs could be detected due to low CQDS content.

In order to explore the morphology and size of the catalyst prepared by the hydrothermal synthesis reaction, the SEM images were recorded. The CQDs/BIS were spherical structure of different sizes with small carbon quantum dots attached to the surface (Fig. 2 a). However, the BMO was a flower-shaped hollow sphere surrounded by irregular flakes (Fig. 2b). In the BMO/CQDs/BIS structure, the BMO was wrapped on the surface of CQDs/BIS, forming a flower-like core–shell structure with the BMO as the shell and CQDs/BIS as the core (Fig. 2c). The TEM images and HRTEM images further showed the internal structure and lattice of the nanocomposite. The TEM test confirmed from another perspective that the catalyst was successfully prepared. The combination of BMO/CQDs/BIS exhibited close contact, in which the complete shell structure on the surface (Fig. 2d-f). The clear lattice distances in HRTEM proved the BIS corresponding to the (130), and (041) crystal planes [35]. And the lattice spacing of 0.315 nm corresponded to the (131) crystal plane of BMO [37]. Those interplane d spacings were corresponded to the mentioned PDF card in XRD results. Besides, the (002) facets of the carbon material lattice could be observed (Fig.S2c), indicating the production of CQDs from waste sludge.

Furthermore, the elemental existence and chemical state of Bi, O, Mo, C, and S were revealed by XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4 a). The distributed peaks in Bi 4f spectra represented at Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 respectively, which located at 159.08 and 164.33 eV (Fig. 4b). It showed the surface chemical element of Bi existed as Bi3+ [38–40]. Moreover, the two peaks at 232.32 and 235.43 eV of BMO/CQDs/BIS samples were associated with Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of Mo3+, respectively (Fig. 4c) [40]. In Fig. 4d, the two main peaks matched well with S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively [41]. The chemical composition O1s signal was deconvoluted into two fitting peaks at the 529.96 and 530.78 eV, which could be related to OBi and OMo bonds, respectively (Fig. 4e) [42]. Finally, the divided peaks of C1s were observed at C-C bond (284.8 eV), CO bond (286.4 eV), and CO bond (288.23 eV), which were attributed to CQDs [38]. Those phenomena proved the stable structure and combined chemical bonds of BMO/CQDs/BIS.

Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed to further confirm the internal chemical structure of the catalyst (Fig. 5 ). The differences in composition and structure between different monomers BIS and BMO and composite catalysts CQDs/BIS and BMO/CQDs/BIS were demonstrated. Evidently, the peaks caused by the OH bond and CO stretching vibration could be found at 3437 and 1630 cm−1 [43]. These broad peaks indicated that the high surface area of the core–shell structure could adsorb a large number of H2O and CO2 during the test process [44]. The absorption band at 1382, 1112, 620, 520 as well as 421 cm−1 were aroused by Bi-S bond vibration [45,46]. The notch at 451 cm−1 was referred as the stretching of the BiO bond [38]. The bending and asymmetric stretching vibration of MoO6 led to absorption peaks at 566 and 734 cm−1, respectively [38]. The vibration of the top oxygen atom in MoO6 was denoted by the achieved peak at 841 cm−1 [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Up and down conversion photoluminescence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PL emission behavior of CQDs was explored by a fluorescence spectrometer (Fig. 6 a). The emission wavelength of CQDs was acquired at 300–500 nm excitation wavelength. In Fig. 6a, there was a small range of redshift in emission peak which reflected down conversion photoluminescence of CQDs. At the 320 nm excitation wavelength, the strongest fluorescence emission band corresponding to 400 nm appeared. This result may be attributed to the dispersion of the size of the CQDs and the different surface states. Passivated smaller carbon dots achieved higher photoluminescence quantum yields [47]. The up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs were presented in Fig. 6b. When the excitation wavelength range was 805–820 nm, the PL spectrum had a strong emission peak near 400 nm, indicating that CQDs have up-conversion photoluminescence. The up and down conversion photoluminescence could covert the multiple photons to suitable emission wavelengths range. The utilization rate of the spectrum could increase to the near-infrared region for full spectrum response [30].

Optical properties were of great significance to the evaluation of the performance of semiconductor materials. The ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of all materials including monomers and composites were shown (Fig. 6c). The maximum absorption band of pure BMO was at 579 nm. Similarly, for a pure BIS with a relatively narrow band gap, its maximum absorption edge was about 748 nm. The BMO/CQDs/BIS appeared tuned light absorption effect, accordingly absorbing more visible and infrared light. This phenomenon indicated that the composite material had a higher light absorption capacity, thus generating more light-generated holes and electrons pairs for photocatalytic degradation.

The photoluminescence spectra of all the prepared samples were tested (excitation λ = 300 nm). It was clearly seen that the maximum emission peaks of all the samples appeared at about 600 nm (Fig. 6d). Compared with all materials, BMO/CQDs/BIS showed obvious fluorescence quenching. This phenomenon revealed that the electron-hole recombination rate of BMO/CQDs/BIS was the lowest. At the same time, the electron-hole recombination rate of CQDs/BIS and BMO/CQDs/BIS was lower than that of the pure BIS, and BMO/BIS. The results indicated the CQDs as electronic conductors and reservoirs to migrate and store photogenerated electrons. The low carrier recombination rate implied that more carriers were qualified to participate in the catalytic reaction, thereby increasing the catalytic efficiency [41].

As shown in Fig. 7 a, in dark conditions, all the as-prepared samples achieved TC adsorption equilibrium in 60 min. Compared with the monomer catalytic materials, the composite catalytic materials BMO/CQDs/BIS could adsorb 20.38% of the pollutant TC. This result showed that the composite catalyst could provide more adsorption sites for pollutant reactions. The results were correlated with the mesoporous structure and specific surface area. The semiconductor material BMO/CQDs/BIS (92.8%) exhibited higher TC degradation efficiency than BIS (20.8%), BMO (63.3%) CQDs/BIS (30.4%) and BMO/BIS (82.3%) (Fig. 7b). The quasi-first order kinetic curve showed that the BMO/CQDs/BIS possessed the highest reaction rate constants of 0.0265 min−1, which was markedly up by 44.8% compared with BMO/BIS (0.0183 min−1) (Fig.S3a and b). Besides the BMO/CQDs/BIS apparent rate constant (0.0265 min−1) was 2.8 and 11.6 times that of BMO and BIS, respectively. This result directly illustrated that the doping of CQDs improved the catalytic performance of the composite catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Various effect factors of photocatalysis reaction
ce:section.ce:para: For further optimizing the catalytic condition, the effect of catalyst dosages (0–1.0 g/L) and initial TC concentrations (10–80 mg/L) were investigated. The dosage of the catalyst had a positive effect on the catalytic reaction (Fig. 7c). Within the scope of 0–0.3 g/L catalyst dosage, the degradation efficiency increased sharply as the catalyst increased. With the 1.0 g/L catalyst dosage, the degradation rate was up to 95.5%. From viewpoint of actual comprehensive benefits, the catalyst dosage of 0.3 g/L was fairly economical option. Meanwhile, as described in Fig. 7d, the high initial concentration of TC also affected the photodegradation reaction. It made it hard for the photons to reach the reaction interface of the catalyst and the photoactive species were reduced. The competition of pollutants and their intermediate products for the catalyst active sites was reinforced [6,48].

The TC degradation rate under different pH (pH = 3–11) was illustrated (Fig. 7e). The removal rate of TC was reduced in an excessively acidic environment or an excessively alkaline environment. And the pH at 3.04 had a stronger inhibitory effect on the catalytic reaction than the alkaline environment. However, the degradation rate was significantly increased when the pH was at 4.5. The adsorption effect of TC by the catalyst was also enhanced due to the increase of pH value, which would hinder the production of light-living oxygen [48]. While pH was equal to 4.5, the TC in the form of a negative molecule could be well combined with the active sites on the catalyst surface to improve the catalytic performance [49]. Besides, the pH was adjusted by 0.1 M NaOH and HCl, in which the pH of the initial reaction solution was at 4.5 without any adjustment. To this end, pH = 4.5 was chosen as the optimal environmental condition for subsequent experiments.

The TC degradation rate of BMO/CQDs/BIS and BMO/BIS with or without optical cut-off filters were evaluated (Fig. 7f). Under the full spectrum (λ > 365 nm), the degradation rates of the catalysts BMO/BIS and BMO/CQDs/BIS were about 94.6% and 95.9%, respectively. In the presence of filters (λ > 420 nm), the degradation rates of the above two catalysts were 82.3% and 92.8%, respectively. It was demonstrated that the photodegradation efficiency of BMO/CQDs/BIS under full spectrum were increased owing to the light conversion of CQDs. In addition, the light conditions of full-spectrum or visible light of BMO/CQDs/BIS were both higher than that of BMO/BIS, indicating that CQDs could improve the electron transfer between heterojunction. The results were consisted with optical property analysis and possible mechanism research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various water quality parameters
ce:section.ce:para: There were many different kinds of anions in the actual water environment that affected the photocatalytic process. The effects of common anions (CO3 2–, SO4 2- and Cl-) on the degradation reaction were shown in Fig. 8 a. When the electrolyte concentration was 0.05 M, the effects of the three electrolytes on the catalytic reaction were ordered as follows: CO3 2–>SO4 2->Cl-. The competitive relationships between the TC molecules and electrolytes on the adsorption sites of BMO/CQD/BIS could bring about a decline in the photodegradation efficiency [48]. Meanwhile, CO3 2– underwent a hydrolysis reaction in water to produce OH–. Excessive OH– was combined with TC pollutants and changed the existence of TC, which made the degradation reaction more difficult. In addition, CO3 2– was also a free radical scavenger [50].

The composition of the actual water body was more complicated. To directly understand the ability of the photocatalyst in the actual water environment, tap water, lake water (taken from Taozi Lake, N28.183°, 112.951°E), and river water (taken from Xiangjiang River, N28.180°, 112.955°E) were used to perform experiments. Besides, the detailed quality parameters of various water sources were illustrated in Table S1. The adsorption rate of pollutants in the actual water was higher than simulated water bodies in laboratory conditions (Fig. 8b), indicating that other substances in the actual water body could adsorb pollutants.

In order to understand the BMO/CQDs/BIS catalyst's ability to treat other types of pollutants, three antibiotics like DOX, OTC, and CIP, and heavy mental like Cr (Ⅵ) were used as experimental objects to test the catalytic efficiency of semiconductor composite materials. The experimental results showed that the BMO/CQDs/BIS catalyst had a degradation rate of 91.6% and 92.5% for DOX and OTC, and the degradation rate for CIP was as high as 80.6% (Fig. 8c). In addition, in the Cr (Ⅵ) wastewater, the catalyst still had a significant reduction effect, which can reach up to 88.2%. The degradation rate of these five pollutants reflected a wide range of applications of this photocatalyst (Fig. 8d). To sum up, compared with previous studies (Table S2), BMO/CQDs/BIS exhibited efficient catalytic capability and broad applicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous removal of TC and Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: In the mixed solution of TC and Cr (VI), BMO/CQDs/BIS simultaneously oxidized or reduced these two pollutants (Fig. 9a and b). For the oxidation process of TC, the degradation rate of TC decreased slightly with the increase of Cr (VI) concentration. The introduction of Cr (VI) affected the formation of some superoxide radicals due to the consumption of electrons. Conversely, the Cr (VI) reduction reaction was accelerated since TC acted as a hole consumer generating massive electrons in the system [51].

The reusability and toxicity of the catalyst are crucial to the practical application [52]. So, the five times photocatalytic cycle experiments of BMO/CQDs/BIS were carried out. In Fig. 9 c and d, the removal rates on the conditions of single TC and the coexistence of TC and Cr6+ were shown respectively. The removal efficiency of the prepared catalysts for the target pollutants decreased slightly during cyclic experiments. The XRD patterns and FT-IR patterns of the catalysts before and after use were shown in Fig.S6. The crystalline structure and functional group structure of the sample after the fifth cycle were similar to the initial sample [53]. The result of leaching of Bi and Mo ion after multiple cycles was shown in Table.S3. The inhibition rate of intermediates against luminescent bacteria was about 20% (Fig.S7a). The low toxicity of by-products was proved. In addition, the biotoxicity of BMO, BMO/BIS and BMO/CQDs/BIS reacted solutions were also tested (Fig.S7b). Among them, the inhibition rate of BMO/CQDs/BIS on E. coli was lower than that of BMO/BIS. This meant that BMO/CQDs/BIS degraded TC more completely than BMO/BIS.

The electron transfer mechanism was an important link in explaining the reaction mechanism. For the purpose of studying the charge conveying pathways in depth, the band structure needed to be calculated. Therefore, the UV–visible diffuse reflection results (Fig. 6c) were referenced to obtain the band gaps (Eg) of BMO, BIS, and CQDs/BIS by following Eq. (1):
(1)
( α h v )
n
= A
h v -
E g
where the direct transition (indirect transition) in the semiconductor photocatalyst, the value of n was 2 (or 0.5), here for BMO and BIS, n = 2 [7]. The optical band gap energy of BMO (2.60 eV), BIS (1.73 eV) and CQDs/BIS (1.62 eV) were determined by linearly extrapolated Tauc plots (Fig. 9a). The band gap energy of the composites reduced significantly due to the CQDs, indicating the improved optical performance.

In addition, the valence band (VB) values of BIS and BMO in VB-XPS spectra were showed in Fig.S8a and b and revealed to be 1.41 and 2.29 eV (vs. NHE) [52]. And the conduction band (CB) positions of BIS and BMO were investigated by and Mott–Schottky plots (Fig. S8c and d). It was evident from Fig. S8 that the flat band potentials (Ef) of BIS and BMO were −0.89 and −0.91 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). After conversion by the Nernst equation [54], the flat-band energies of BIS and BMO were determined as −0.28 and −0.30 V (vs. NHE). From the positive slope of the C-2 curve, both BIS and BMO were n-type semiconductors, and the flat-band potential (Ef) of n-type semiconductors was close to the Fermi level [55]. Therefore, the Fermi level of BIS and BMO were −0.28 and −0.30 eV (vs. NHE) approximately. According to the valence band position and band gap value mentioned above, the BIS and BMO conduction band potentials were finally determined as −0.29 and −0.32 eV, respectively.

Through the above analysis, the band structure of the BMO/CQDs/BIS heterojunctions was shown in Fig. 9b. The BMO/CQDs/BIS was proved to be Type Ⅰ heterojunctions [56]. The band edge of BMO stretched across the high oxidation potential (<O2/
O2 –-0.28 eV/ NHE at pH 7) and reduction potential (>H2O/•OH 2.27 eV /NHE at pH 7) [6]. The aroused electron on BMO would transfer to CB of BIS according to thermodynamical equilibrium. Thanks to CQDs, the electron located at interface of the heterojunctions could be guided from BMO to BIS, preventing electrons from accumulating [57]. The CQDs sandwiched between BMO and BIS was capable of dispersing and transferring excessive electrons and holes on BIS, reducing the recombination of photocarriers and photo corrosion.

As was discussed above, the capacity of BIS high light absorption and the properties of CQDs up and down conversion photoluminescence increased the utilization efficiency of the full spectrum. At the same time, due to the existence of conjugated systems, CQDs could serve as electron reservoirs and acceptors in the process of photocatalytic reactions, thereby accelerating charge migration and preventing electron-hole pairs from recombining. The charge separation efficiency could be well verified by photocurrent response (PC), electrochemical impedance spectra as well as PL test. The PL analysis was discussed in the previous section.

In Fig. 10 c, BMO/CQDs/BIS had the highest photocurrent intensity, followed by CQDs/BIS. The photocurrent intensity of the semiconductor materials introduced with CQDs was higher than that of the remaining materials. It was proved that CQDs not only could effectively increase the number of electrons but boost photogenerated charges separating. The EIS illustrated the interface electron transfer rate of different materials. After extensive analysis and comparisons, BMO/CQDs/BIS had the smallest arc radius, indicating that this material has the fastest charge transfer rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active species generation and reaction attack sites
ce:section.ce:para:
The different active free radicals contributed to the photodegradation process. The three active species (
O2 –, h+ and
OH) could be captured by 1 mM 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), EDTA-2Na, and isopropanol (IPA). The degradation rate of TC had been observably decreased with the scavengers (Fig. 11 a). Under the effect of BQ and EDTA-2Na free radical scavengers, the degradation rate of TC decreased to 39 % and 29.2%. This data proved that
O2 – and h+ contributed the most to the redox process of TC. The degradation rate of TC also dropped by 20% in the existence of IPA, which reflected
OH also participated in the pollutant degradation process. In addition, the N2 stripping experiment was used to confirm the role of dissolved oxygen to further explain the
O2 – generation. The experiment proved that after the dissolved oxygen was removed, the degradation effect of TC was reduced to 25.9%. It was proved that dissolved oxygen can scavenge photo-generated electrons in the solution to generate
O2 – radicals to accelerate the degradation reaction. In short, the results showed that three active radicals could be devoted to the catalytic process.

In Fig. 11b, sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8) was a kind of electron trap, the solution with Na2S2O8 added had the lowest removal rate of Cr (VI), indicating that e- was the main active species for Cr (VI) reduction. Besides, the addition of IPA and BQ as
OH and
O2 – trapping agent had few effects on the reaction. Interestingly, Cr (VI) reduction rate was improved after the hole trapping agent EDTA-2Na was introduced into the system. It was found that the electrons were increased significantly due to the large consumption of holes in the system.

To confirm the above conclusions, ESR electron spin capture technology was implemented. DMPO was used as spin traps to capture
O2 –,
OH. In Fig. 11c, no signal of the
OH appeared in the absence of light. After lighting, the obvious characteristic peak of
OH 1:2:2:1 four-line spectrum was observed [58]. At the same time, the situation of
O2 – was similar to that of
OH, and
O2 – was generated after light irradiation, indicating that these two active free radicals were produced during the light reaction (Fig. 11d). Hence, h+ remained on the VB of BIS and BMO to degrade pollutants and generate
OH. The results consisted with charge transfer properties.

Evidently, the electron transfer route was identified in the mechanism of the band structure. The BMO/CQDs/BIS was formed by a core–shell heterojunctions with a Type Ⅰ energy band. [59]. Therefore, a detailed analysis of the photodegradation mechanism was exhibited (Scheme 2 ). The BIS and BMO semiconductors were both excited to generate more electron-hole pairs due to the photoluminescence of CQDs. Then, the electrons of BMO flowed to BIS, while the holes remained on BMO and BIS with more positive potential to oxidation of pollutants [60]. The electron-hole pairs were easily separated due to the excellent electron storage characteristics of CQDs. At the same time, the electron could migrate to the surface of the core–shell structure, generating
O2 – and photoreduction [42]. The hole on the shell BMO activated H2O to form of
OH [61]. Finally, the efficient oxidation of pollutants was ascribed by a large number of active species and h+ from BMO/CQDs/BIS. The reaction attack sites of optimized TC molecules were predicted by the calculation of the HOMO and LUMO FED (Fig.S4). The contribution values on the atom were detailed in Table 2 , corresponding to the theoretical calculation. The atoms at O (25), C (8), N (30) and O (23) with higher 2FED2 HOMO values indicated that these unsaturated sites were easily oxidized by photogenerated holes. Meanwhile, the positions of N (30), C (15), O (25), N (21), C (8) with higher FED2 HOMO + FED2 LUMO values indicated that the reaction sites were prefer to be attacked by
O2 – [62]. The results were further verified in possible degradation pathway of TC.

According to the intermediate products during the photodegradation process, the possible mineralization pathway of antibiotics could be deduced by LC-MS. During the illumination, the intensity of the peaks of different by-products gradually increased, while the peaks of initial TC decreased significantly (Fig.S5). This explained that TC was oxidized and reduced to other small molecular compounds under the action of the catalyst, which proved that TC could be mineralized. The corresponding intermediate product conversion process was shown in Fig. 12 . According to the by-products measured by LC-MS, three degradation pathways of TC were presented. In the first step, hydroxylation reaction, demethylation reaction, and double bond cleavage and formation occurred, resulting in intermediate products with m/z of 476 (P1), 417 (P8) [15], and 432 (P12). In pathway 1 [63], P1 would remove the two methyl groups on the N atom under the attack of active free radicals to form P2 (m/z = 393). After further reaction of P2, the carbon–oxygen double bond was broken, and the amide group was removed to form P2. P3 (m/z = 344). Then, after the de-hydroxylation reaction and ring-opening reaction, the intermediate products P5 (m/z = 327) and P6 (m/z = 283) produced P7 (m/z = 217) with a smaller molecular mass. Pathway 2: P8 removed the amide group and amino group to form P9 (m/z = 362) [64], and then underwent ring-opening reaction and de-hydroxylation reaction to form P6. Pathway 3: Under the attack of superoxide radicals, P12 (m/z = 432) [65] opened the ring to deamidate to generate P13 (m/z = 437), and then the continuous double bond broke to form the product P16 (m/z = 216). It was observed that these by-products with a molecular weight>200 were finally transformed into small molecular organic compounds such as m/z = 158; 130; 102; 98, which were finally mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

The BMO/CQDs/BIS were successfully prepared by hydrothermal and solvothermal methods. After characterization experiment, the results showed that BMO/CQDs/BIS was a core–shell heterojunctions structure. The narrow bandgap of BIS was combined with BMO to construct a suitable band structure with high redox ability. The CQDs sandwiched BMO/BIS heterointerface assisted in more photoinduced electrons and holes generation and separation. Besides, the up and down conversion photoluminescence of CQDs could effectively widen the absorption spectra range to the ultraviolet and near-infrared light region. Moreover, the special structure of BMO/CQDs/BIS had a higher specific surface area and better catalytic efficiency. Meanwhile, the excellent photocatalytic performance was exhibited by oxidating various persistent organic pollutants and reducing heavy metals. The optimal reaction conditions of BMO/CQDs/BIS were confirmed at pH = 4.5 and a low dosage (10–30 mg/L). Especially, the obtained catalyst had certain efficiency and stability for the degradation behavior of TC in real water quality. Finally, the reaction mechanism in this photocatalysis process was proved by the charge transfer route and possible degradation pathway of TC. The organic pollutants could be mineralized efficiently. This economic and efficient photocatalysis would be a facile technology in the resourcelization of waste sludge and the simultaneous water purification.
